title: Demystifying Tacit Knowledge in Graphic Design: Characteristics, Instances, Approaches, and Guidelines
authors: Son, Kihoon and Choi, DaEun and Kim, Tae Soo and Kim, Juho
abstract: Despite the growing demand for professional graphic design knowledge, the tacit nature of design inhibits knowledge sharing. However, there is a limited understanding on the characteristics and instances of tacit knowledge in graphic design. In this work, we build a comprehensive set of tacit knowledge characteristics through a literature review. Through interviews with 10 professional graphic designers, we collected 123 tacit knowledge instances and labeled their characteristics. By qualitatively coding the instances, we identified the prominent elements, actions, and purposes of tacit knowledge. To identify which instances have been addressed the least, we conducted a systematic literature review of prior system support to graphic design. By understanding the reasons for the lack of support on these instances based on their characteristics, we propose design guidelines for capturing and applying tacit knowledge in design tools. This work takes a step towards understanding tacit knowledge, and how this knowledge can be communicated.
venue: Proceedings of the 2024 CHI Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems
primary_reason: Builds a comprehensive set of tacit knowledge characteristics via literature review and characterizes/defines tacit design knowledge in graphic design, satisfying C2 and likely C3.
val_reason: The paper develops a VR Deceptive Game Design Assessment Guide based on a critical literature review, which indicates synthesis/categorization of design patterns, but the abstract does not clearly state an explicit adapted or original definition.

cluster_summary_candidate: This cluster synthesizes prior work on design principles, emphasizing how a design-knowledge construct is made explicit, organized, or put to work rather than only where it is applied. Conceptually, it treats design principles as conceptual framework. Related forms include Tacit Design Knowledge, Design Knowledge. The shared move also involves definition and conceptualization and capture and transfer. Its contribution pattern is coded as Methodological, Artifact/System, Theoretical. Application and method terms are treated as supporting facets, especially methods or lenses such as Case Study, Prototype, Participatory Design and contexts such as Education / Learning, Workplace. Representative papers include From interaction to trajectories: designing coherent journeys through user experiences; Generating implications for design through design research.

title: Embracing Non-Natural Design in Human-Computer Interaction
authors: Vatavu, Radu-Daniel
abstract: Decades ago, the natural interaction design paradigm has brought about a revolutionary shift in perspective regarding how interactions with computer systems could and should take place. By promoting intuition, familiarity, and context matching, natural interactions leverage users‚Äô best capabilities to engage in effective dialogue with their computer interlocutors. Natural interaction has prevailed and transpires today in the way we use gestures, voice, or eye gaze to interact with mobile and wearable devices, communicate with personal assistants, play video games through whole-body movements, and engage with public ambient displays. However, recent contexts of use, represented by novel extended reality environments, computer systems designed to augment the human body, and creative designs of assistive technology, have resulted in interactions that can no longer be characterized as ‚Äúnatural.‚Äù In fact, the opposite of natural is true for them. An interaction technique for retrieving digital files from thin air, without any feedback other than within users‚Äô own imagination, does not follow natural design guidelines, but can nevertheless prove effective. Alternating between normal and whispered voices is not a natural speech behavior, yet it enables effective discrimination between commands addressed to the system and actual speech to be transformed into text input. Eyelid gestures, reproducing movement patterns, are not natural for how we normally close and open our eyes, but are effective input for users with motor impairments. In this context, I will discuss non-natural interaction design [5], a transformative and creative process in Human-Computer Interaction research and practice that leads to highly usable and effective interactions with computer systems by deliberately deviating from users‚Äô expectations and their experience of engaging with the natural, physical world. Non-natural interactions are surprising, unexpected, thought-provoking and, by design, non natural. Yet, they foster usability, accessibility, and efficiency for users. In my talk, I will exemplify the paradigm of non-natural interaction design through practical use cases developed and examined in my lab, spanning from wearable devices that challenge user agency [1] to smart buildings featuring invisible layers of digital content anchored in thin air [3], augmenting and extending the human body with virtual limbs [4], and computer-supported worlds that enable switching between different reality augmentations [2].

cluster_summary_candidate: This cluster synthesizes prior work on design principles, emphasizing how a design-knowledge construct is made explicit, organized, or put to work rather than only where it is applied. Conceptually, it treats design principles as conceptual framework. Related forms include Design Knowledge, Design Theory. The shared move also involves definition and conceptualization and representation and documentation. Its contribution pattern is coded as Methodological, Artifact/System, Theoretical. Application and method terms are treated as supporting facets, especially methods or lenses such as Design Science, Prototype, Ontology and contexts such as Education / Learning, Industrial Design. Representative papers include Design-oriented human-computer interaction; A generic model for reflective design.

title: Don't Do That! Hunting Down Visual Design Smells in Complex UIs against Design Guidelines
authors: Yang, Bo and Xing, Zhenchang and Xia, Xin and Chen, Chunyang and Ye, Deheng and Li, Shanping
abstract: Just like code smells in source code, UI design has visual design smells. We study 93 don't-do-that guidelines in the Material Design, a complex design system created by Google. We find that these don't-guidelines go far beyond UI aesthetics, and involve seven general design dimensions (layout, typography, iconography, navigation, communication, color, and shape) and four component design aspects (anatomy, placement, behavior, and usage). Violating these guidelines results in visual design smells in UIs (or UI design smells). In a study of 60,756 UIs of 9,286 Android apps, we find that 7,497 UIs of 2,587 apps have at least one violation of some Material Design guidelines. This reveals the lack of developer training and tool support to avoid UI design smells. To fill this gap, we design an automated UI design smell detector (UIS-Hunter) that extracts and validates multi-modal UI information (component metadata, typography, iconography, color, and edge) for detecting the violation of diverse don't-guidelines in Material Design. The detection accuracy of UIS-Hunter is high (precision=0.81, recall=0.90) on the 60,756 UIs of 9,286 apps. We build a guideline gallery with real-world UI design smells that UIS-Hunter detects for developers to learn the best Material Design practices. Our user studies show that UIS-Hunter is more effective than manual detection of UI design smells, and the UI design smells that are detected by UIS-Hunter have severely negative impacts on app users.

Abstract—Just like code smells in source code, UI design has visual design smells. We study 93 don(cid:1)t-do-that guidelines in the Material Design, a complex design system created by Google. We find that these don(cid:1)t-guidelines go far beyond UI aesthetics, and involve seven general design dimensions (layout, typography, iconography, navigation, communication, color, and shape) and four component design aspects (anatomy, placement, behavior, and usage). Violating these guidelines results in visual design smells in UIs (or UI design smells). In a study of 60,756 UIs of 9,286 Android apps, we find that 7,497 UIs of 2,587 apps have at least one violation of some Material Design guidelines. This reveals the lack of developer training and tool support to avoid UI design smells. To fill this gap, we design an automated UI design smell detector (UIS-Hunter) that extracts and validates multi-modal UI information (component metadata, typography, iconography, color, and edge) for detecting the violation of diverse don(cid:1)t-guidelines in Material Design.

cluster_summary_candidate: This cluster synthesizes prior work on design principles, emphasizing how a design-knowledge construct is made explicit, organized, or put to work rather than only where it is applied. Conceptually, it treats design principles as conceptual framework. Related forms include Design Knowledge, Design Theory. The shared move also involves definition and conceptualization and representation and documentation. Its contribution pattern is coded as Methodological, Artifact/System, Theoretical. Application and method terms are treated as supporting facets, especially methods or lenses such as Design Science, Prototype, Ontology and contexts such as Education / Learning, Industrial Design. Representative papers include Design-oriented human-computer interaction; A generic model for reflective design.

title: The Rough Mile: Testing a Framework of Immersive Practice
authors: Spence, Jocelyn and Hazzard, Adrian and McGrath, Sean and Greenhalgh, Chris and Benford, Steve
abstract: We present our case study on gifting digital music, The Rough Mile, as an example of a Framework of Immersive Practice, intended for researchers and practitioners in HCI and interaction design. Although immersion is a frequently used term in the HCI and related literatures, we find no definitions or frameworks that are robust enough to capture the full range of multi-sensory, emotional, and cognitive engagement that the richest of these experiences can entail. We therefore turn to the theatrical performance literature to distil a theory-based framework of practices that can apply to interdisciplinary projects as well as works with an entirely aesthetic aim. The design choices and findings of The Rough Mile are presented in terms of this framework, leading to a discussion of the design guidelines that can shape its use in any HCI or interaction design project aiming for a deep, personal engagement through technology.
venue: Proceedings of the 2017 Conference on Designing Interactive Systems
primary_reason: Distills a theory-based framework of immersive practice from theatrical literature for HCI design and notes absence of robust existing definitions, suggesting synthesis and its own framework definition.
val_reason: The paper explicitly distils a theory-based framework from theatrical performance literature and states that existing definitions/frameworks are inadequate, indicating both synthesis and an adapted or newly articulated framework/definition for immersive practice.

cluster_summary_candidate: This cluster synthesizes prior work on design principles, emphasizing how a design-knowledge construct is made explicit, organized, or put to work rather than only where it is applied. Conceptually, it treats design principles as conceptual framework. Related forms include Tacit Design Knowledge, Design Knowledge. The shared move also involves definition and conceptualization and capture and transfer. Its contribution pattern is coded as Methodological, Artifact/System, Theoretical. Application and method terms are treated as supporting facets, especially methods or lenses such as Case Study, Prototype, Participatory Design and contexts such as Education / Learning, Workplace. Representative papers include From interaction to trajectories: designing coherent journeys through user experiences; Generating implications for design through design research.

title: From interaction to trajectories: designing coherent journeys through user experiences
authors: Benford, Steve and Giannachi, Gabriella and Koleva, Boriana and Rodden, Tom
abstract: The idea of interactional trajectories through interfaces has emerged as a sensitizing concept from recent studies of tangible interfaces and interaction in museums and galleries. We put this concept to work as a lens to reflect on published studies of complex user experiences that extend over space and time and involve multiple roles and interfaces. We develop a conceptual framework in which trajectories explain these user experiences as journeys through hybrid structures, punctuated by transitions, and in which interactivity and collaboration are orchestrated. Our framework is intended to sensitize future studies, help distill craft knowledge into design guidelines and patterns, identify technology requirements, and provide a boundary object to connect HCI with Performance Studies.
venue: Proceedings of the SIGCHI Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems
primary_reason: Develops a conceptual framework of trajectories by reflecting on published studies, synthesizing prior work and defining the construct, satisfying C2 and likely C3.
val_reason: Uses the concept of interactional trajectories to reflect on published studies and develops a conceptual framework synthesizing prior work, but the abstract does not clearly state an explicit original or adapted definition, so full text is needed for C3.

cluster_summary_candidate: This cluster synthesizes prior work on design principles, emphasizing how a design-knowledge construct is made explicit, organized, or put to work rather than only where it is applied. Conceptually, it treats design principles as conceptual framework. Related forms include Tacit Design Knowledge, Design Knowledge. The shared move also involves definition and conceptualization and capture and transfer. Its contribution pattern is coded as Methodological, Artifact/System, Theoretical. Application and method terms are treated as supporting facets, especially methods or lenses such as Case Study, Prototype, Participatory Design and contexts such as Education / Learning, Workplace. Representative papers include From interaction to trajectories: designing coherent journeys through user experiences; Generating implications for design through design research.

title: Design Heuristics for Web-Based Severe Weather Risk Communication
authors: Jit, Sophia and Leiva, Amanda and Bicksler, Rebecca and Soden, Robert
abstract: Weather risk communication is an interdisciplinary field of research that aims to understand how to communicate complex risk information related to extreme weather events to the general public to assist with safety-related decision-making. Web-based tools are increasingly important in risk communication, but the complexity of the topic creates difficulties for designers without prior domain experience. To help address this challenge, we used key findings from relevant scholarly literature to adapt Nielsen‚Äôs usability heuristics for use by designers working on web-based risk communication tools. We then evaluated and iterated on these heuristics through research with both risk communication experts and designers. Based on our results, we argue that the final set of heuristics we present in this paper are useful for supporting designers in identifying and addressing usability issues in web-based tools aimed at communicating severe weather risk information to the public.
venue: Proceedings of the 2025 ACM SIGCAS/SIGCHI Conference on Computing and Sustainable Societies
primary_reason: Adapts Nielsen's usability heuristics from scholarly literature into design heuristics for a new domain, satisfying both C2 (synthesis) and C3 (adaptation of an existing construct definition).
val_reason: The paper adapts Nielsen‚Äôs existing usability heuristics for a specific design context using findings from relevant scholarly literature, so it both synthesizes prior work and explicitly adapts a design-knowledge construct.

distinguishing_evidence_terms: Target Oriented, Web Severe Weather, Heuristic Evaluation, Standardized Notation
cluster_summary_candidate: This cluster defines and conceptualizes design heuristics, emphasizing how a design-knowledge construct is made explicit, organized, or put to work rather than only where it is applied. Conceptually, it treats design heuristics as evaluative design criteria. Related forms include Design Principles, Design Guidelines. The shared move also involves synthesis of prior work and representation and documentation. Its contribution pattern is coded as Artifact/System, Methodological, Theoretical. Application and method terms are treated as supporting facets, especially methods or lenses such as Heuristic Evaluation, Interview and contexts such as Education / Learning, Accessibility. Representative papers include Revised set of usability heuristics for the evaluation of interactive systems; Introduction of a standardized Notation of Design Heuristics for Knowledge Formalization.

title: From human factors to human actors to human crafters
authors: Maceli, Monica and Atwood, Michael
abstract: Meta-design theory emphasizes that future use can never be entirely anticipated at design time, as users shape their environments in response to emerging needs; systems should therefore be designed to adapt to future conditions in the hands of end users. For most of human history, all design was meta-design; designers were also users, and the environments of design and use were one and the same. Technology introduced a divide between the skilled producers and unskilled consumers of technology, and between design time and use time. In our increasingly complex technological environments, tomorrow's meta-designers must be able to anticipate the environment in which the end users will work in order to provide the flexibility for users to craft their tools. By exploring and projecting forward current trends in technology use, we have identified key principles for meta-designers and suggest that using them as design heuristics will aid meta-designers in crafting systems for future end-users.
venue: Proceedings of the 2011 IConference
primary_reason: Discusses meta-design theory and articulates what meta-design means, plausibly synthesizing and defining a design-knowledge construct; flagged to verify.
val_reason: This paper discusses a framework for experience design and design heuristics grounded in analysis of a mediascape experience, but the abstract does not clearly show synthesis from prior literature or an explicit definition, so full text is needed.

cluster_label_candidate: Design Principles as Synthesized Conceptual Framework
distinguishing_evidence_terms: Case Study, Human Actors, Role Playing, Dark Patterns
cluster_summary_candidate: This cluster synthesizes prior work on design principles, emphasizing how a design-knowledge construct is made explicit, organized, or put to work rather than only where it is applied. Conceptually, it treats design principles as conceptual framework. Related forms include Tacit Design Knowledge, Design Knowledge. The shared move also involves definition and conceptualization and capture and transfer. Its contribution pattern is coded as Methodological, Artifact/System, Theoretical. Application and method terms are treated as supporting facets, especially methods or lenses such as Case Study, Prototype, Participatory Design and contexts such as Education / Learning, Workplace. Representative papers include From interaction to trajectories: designing coherent journeys through user experiences; Generating implications for design through design research.

title: Towards Creating a Body of Evidence-based Interactive Digital Narrative Design Knowledge: Approaches and Challenges
authors: Roth, Christian and Koenitz, Hartmut
abstract: In the context of this paper we take design knowledge as the methods applied by the creators (for example game designers in the case of games) of interactive experiences in contrast to for example the design of authoring tools or the design of game engines and similar computational systems. Such knowledge about rule-based game design is widespread as evidenced by numerous book publications and many university-level programs of study. In contrast, there is considerable less academic or professional knowledge on the design of interactive narratives -- for example, book publications focused on this topic number in the single digits and in academic programs the topic only exists on the margins. This paper proposes the use of empirical research methods as means to address this situation and examines specific challenges. On this foundation, we introduce a research effort to gather transferable interactive narrative design knowledge and report on a first user study. Finally, we provide guidelines and discuss future research.
venue: Proceedings of the 2nd International Workshop on Multimedia Alternate Realities
primary_reason: Provides its own definition of design knowledge (methods applied by creators) and works toward synthesizing a body of interactive digital narrative design knowledge.
val_reason: The truncated abstract centers on teaching object-oriented design knowledge and related constructs like patterns, but does not clearly show whether it synthesizes or defines them, so full text is needed.

cluster_label_candidate: Design Frameworks as Conceptual Framework
distinguishing_evidence_terms: Systematic Review, Framework Holistic, Entertainment Games, Experience Dynamics
cluster_summary_candidate: This cluster defines and conceptualizes design frameworks, emphasizing how a design-knowledge construct is made explicit, organized, or put to work rather than only where it is applied. Conceptually, it treats design frameworks as conceptual framework. Related forms include Design Knowledge, Design Principles. The shared move also involves synthesis of prior work and evidence-based evaluation. Its contribution pattern is coded as Methodological, Empirical, Artifact/System. Application and method terms are treated as supporting facets, especially methods or lenses such as User-centered Design, Systematic Review, Interview and contexts such as Education / Learning. Representative papers include The experience, dynamics and artifacts framework: towards a holistic model for designing serious and entertainment games; The Experience, Dynamics and Artifacts Framework: Towards a Holistic Model for Designing Serious and Entertainment Games.

title: The Dark Patterns Knowledge Stack: Exploring New Ways to Negotiate Context, Law, and Design
authors: Gray, Colin M.
abstract: Research on dark patterns has grown rapidly, but challenges remain in situating these practices within broader socio-technical, legal, and design contexts. In this essay, I introduce the concept of the ‚Äúdark patterns knowledge stack‚Äù as a new way of synthesizing evidence about manipulative, coercive, and deceptive design practices. Inspired by Alexander‚Äôs notion of pattern language, I demonstrate how the knowledge stack aligns multiple layers of analysis and evidence‚Äîfrom interfaces and user characteristics to the socio-technical landscape and user intentions‚Äîrevealing how manipulative practices interrelate across scales, are perpetuated through key business metrics, and evolve over time. Use of the knowledge stack is demonstrated through two case studies, followed by provocations for scholars, regulators, and practitioners to work together to more effectively identify harms, negotiate accountability, and chart pathways for more just and transparent digital systems.
venue: Proceedings of the 2026 CHI Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems
primary_reason: Essay synthesizing dark patterns evidence into a 'knowledge stack' concept inspired by pattern language, defining its own framework, plausibly satisfying C2/C3 but warrants full-text confirmation.
val_reason: Studies tacit knowledge transmission through game design practice in a small team, but the abstract does not clearly indicate a synthesis from prior literature or an explicit definition of the construct.

title: Toward an Inventory of Plans and Patterns, and Their Use to Foster Strategic Knowledge in Programming Novices
authors: Costantini, Umberto and Lonati, Violetta and Morpurgo, Anna
abstract: When learning to program, different types of knowledge and skills have to be built: syntactic knowledge, i.e., knowledge of the language features; conceptual knowledge, i.e., knowledge of the semantics of the various constructs; strategic knowledge, i.e. the ability to use syntactic and conceptual knowledge in the most appropriate and effective way to solve programming problems. Strategic knowledge also includes the ability to exploit and combine solutions to known problems to solve new ones and is one of the hardest skills to acquire when learning to programm [5, 6, 11]. Terms such as goals[10] patterns [5, 6, 11]. schema,[9] strategies[4], and variables' roles[3] have been used to refer to this kind of knowledge. Several authors suggest that strategic knowledge should be taught explicitly, however there are three main issues to address: plans/patterns/schema are used sometimes interchangeably and sometimes similarly but not correspondingly; it is claimed that a small number of plans/patterns is needed in an introductory programming course, however there is no agreed upon inventory of plans/patterns among the CS community;the notion of variables' roles isn't always explicitly linked to the notion of plans/patterns;little instructional material is available that explicitly teaches strategies, and it is neither easy nor obvious how to design such material. The aim of this study is to investigate whether and how such notions of plans/patterns/schema and variables' roles can be integrated into introductory programming courses.The idea is to establish a framework that includes and arranges the different definitions presented in literature. The framework developed will be used for building a catalogue of plans/pattern and variables' roles encountered in CS1 practice, in CS1 assessment, and in the literature on introductory programming; this catalogue will be the basis for designing new instructional/learning material for fostering strategic knowledge. The developed material will be used in one of our introductory programming courses and in peer tutoring activities for the students. The effectiveness will be tested evaluating students programming skills with a pre-test and a post-test, using a group as a treatment group and another one as control group.
venue: Proceedings of the 2020 ACM Conference on International Computing Education Research

title: Designerly Ways of Knowing in HRI: Broadening the Scope of Design-oriented HRI Through the Concept of Intermediate-level Knowledge
authors: Lupetti, Maria Luce and Zaga, Cristina and Cila, Nazli
abstract: Interest in design methods and tools has been steadily growing in HRI. Yet, design is not acknowledged as a discipline with specific epistemology and methodology. Designerly HRI work is validated through user studies which, we argue, provide a limited account of the knowledge design produces. This paper aims to broaden current understanding of designerly HRI work and its contributions by unpacking what designerly knowledge is and how to produce it. Through a critical analysis of current HRI design literature, we identify a lack of work dedicated to understanding the conceptual implications of robotic artifacts. These, in fact, are implicit carriers of crucial HRI knowledge that can challenge established assumptions about how a robot should look, act, and be like. We conclude by discussing a set of practices desirable to legitimize designerly HRI work, and calling for further research addressing the conceptual implications designerly HRI work.
venue: Proceedings of the 2021 ACM/IEEE International Conference on Human-Robot Interaction
primary_reason: Critically analyzes HRI design literature to unpack what designerly/intermediate-level knowledge is, synthesizing and defining design-knowledge constructs (C2 and C3).
val_reason: It critically analyzes HRI design literature to unpack designerly knowledge and intermediate-level knowledge, indicating a synthesis/categorization of design-knowledge constructs, but any explicit adapted or original definition is not clearly visible in the truncated abstract.

cluster_label_candidate: Design Heuristics as Evaluative Design Criteria
distinguishing_evidence_terms: Target Oriented, Web Severe Weather, Heuristic Evaluation, Standardized Notation
cluster_summary_candidate: This cluster defines and conceptualizes design heuristics, emphasizing how a design-knowledge construct is made explicit, organized, or put to work rather than only where it is applied. Conceptually, it treats design heuristics as evaluative design criteria. Related forms include Design Principles, Design Guidelines. The shared move also involves synthesis of prior work and representation and documentation. Its contribution pattern is coded as Artifact/System, Methodological, Theoretical. Application and method terms are treated as supporting facets, especially methods or lenses such as Heuristic Evaluation, Interview and contexts such as Education / Learning, Accessibility. Representative papers include Revised set of usability heuristics for the evaluation of interactive systems; Introduction of a standardized Notation of Design Heuristics for Knowledge Formalization.

title: Let's Get Divorced: Constructing Knowledge Outcomes for Critical Design and Constructive Design Research
authors: Forlizzi, Jodi and Zimmerman, John and Hekkert, Paul and Koskinen, Ilpo
abstract: Over the last two decades, constructive design research (CDR) - more commonly called Research through Design within HCI - has become an accepted mode of scholarly inquiry within the design research community. It has been described as having three distinct genres: lab, field, and showroom. The lab and field genres typically take a pragmatic stance and typically propose a preferred future. Research done following the showroom approach - more commonly known as critical design (CD), speculative design, or design fictions - typically offers a polemic and a critique of the current state embodied in an artifact. Recently, we have observed a growing conflict within the design research community between pragmatic and critical design researchers [4]. To help reduce this conflict, we called for a divorce between CD and pragmatic CDR, advocating that each approach has its own merits and should be evaluated on its own account. Other design researchers have pushed back on this stance, seeking to create some middle ground to connect these two types of research. In this day-long workshop, we seek to look at exemplar CDR and CD case studies, to develop methods for describing, evaluating, replicating, and making use of knowledge outcomes from these two forms of design research.
venue: Proceedings of the 2018 ACM Conference Companion Publication on Designing Interactive Systems
primary_reason: Discusses and constructs knowledge outcomes for constructive and critical design research genres, synthesizing and defining design research constructs, satisfying C2 and likely C3.
val_reason: This appears to discuss knowledge outcomes in critical and constructive design research, plausibly concerning design knowledge, but the abstract excerpt does not clearly confirm a synthesis or explicit definition.

cluster_label_candidate: Design Principles as Synthesized Conceptual Framework
distinguishing_evidence_terms: Case Study, Human Actors, Role Playing, Dark Patterns
cluster_summary_candidate: This cluster synthesizes prior work on design principles, emphasizing how a design-knowledge construct is made explicit, organized, or put to work rather than only where it is applied. Conceptually, it treats design principles as conceptual framework. Related forms include Tacit Design Knowledge, Design Knowledge. The shared move also involves definition and conceptualization and capture and transfer. Its contribution pattern is coded as Methodological, Artifact/System, Theoretical. Application and method terms are treated as supporting facets, especially methods or lenses such as Case Study, Prototype, Participatory Design and contexts such as Education / Learning, Workplace. Representative papers include From interaction to trajectories: designing coherent journeys through user experiences; Generating implications for design through design research.

title: Immodest Proposals: Research Through Design and Knowledge
authors: Bardzell, Jeffrey and Bardzell, Shaowen and Koefoed Hansen, Lone
abstract: The paper offers theoretical support for research through design (RtD) by arguing that in order to legitimize and make use of research through design as research, HCI researchers need to explore and clarify how RtD objects might contribute to knowledge. Leveraging the tradition of aesthetics in the arts and humanities, we argue that while the intentions of the object's designer are important and while annotations are a good mechanism to articulate them, the critical reception of objects is equally foundational to RtD's broader knowledge impacts within HCI. Such a scholarly critical reception is needed precisely because of the potential inexhaustibility of design objects' meanings; their inability to be paraphrased simply and adequately. Offering a multilevel analysis of the (critical) design fiction Menstruation Machine by Sputniko!, the paper explores how design objects co-produce knowledge, by working through complex design problem spaces in non-reductive ways, proposing new connections and distinctions, and embodying design ideas and processes across time and minds.
venue: Proceedings of the 33rd Annual ACM Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems
primary_reason: Offers theoretical support for research through design and clarifies how RtD objects contribute to knowledge, engaging with design knowledge as topic and likely defining constructs.
val_reason: The paper centrally concerns knowledge contributions of research through design and appears to theorize how RtD objects contribute to knowledge, but the truncated abstract does not clearly show a synthesis from prior work or an explicit definition, so full-text review is needed.

cluster_summary_candidate: This cluster synthesizes prior work on design principles, emphasizing how a design-knowledge construct is made explicit, organized, or put to work rather than only where it is applied. Conceptually, it treats design principles as conceptual framework. Related forms include Tacit Design Knowledge, Design Knowledge. The shared move also involves definition and conceptualization and capture and transfer. Its contribution pattern is coded as Methodological, Artifact/System, Theoretical. Application and method terms are treated as supporting facets, especially methods or lenses such as Case Study, Prototype, Participatory Design and contexts such as Education / Learning, Workplace. Representative papers include From interaction to trajectories: designing coherent journeys through user experiences; Generating implications for design through design research.

title: Surveying a Landscape of Ethics-Focused Design Methods
authors: Chivukula, Shruthi Sai and Gray, Colin and Li, Ziqing and Pivonka, Anne C. and Chen, Jingning
abstract: Over the past decade, HCI researchers and practitioners have increasingly addressed ethics-focused issues through a range of theoretical, methodological, and pragmatic contributions to the field. While many forms of design knowledge have been proposed and described, we focus explicitly on knowledge that has been codified as ‚Äúmethods,‚Äù which we define as structured supports for everyday work practices of designers. In this article, we identify, analyze, and map a collection of 63 existing methods intentionally designed for ethical impact. Building on results of a content analysis of these methods, we contribute a descriptive record of how these methods operationalize ethics, their intended audience or context of use, their ‚Äúcore‚Äù or ‚Äúscript,‚Äù and the means by which these methods are formulated and codified. Building on these results, we provide an initial definition of ethics-focused methods, identifying potential opportunities for the development of future methods to support ethical design practice.
venue: ACM J. Responsib. Comput.
primary_reason: Surveys and maps 63 ethics-focused design methods via content analysis (C2) and explicitly defines methods as structured supports for designers' work practices (C3).
val_reason: The abstract reports three design methods for AR in a historical site, but it is unclear from the provided text whether these are synthesized from prior work or explicitly defined as design-knowledge constructs, warranting full-text review.

Surveying a Landscape of Ethics-Focused Design Methods 22:5 ontologies [ 165 ]), levels of instigation of design knowledge in design activity [ 83 , 109 ], and different types of design knowledge [ 114 , 115 ]. For the purpose of this article, we explore “methods” as a particular form of design knowledge that enables “the creation of design states” [ 109 ] that support and advance a designer’s capability [ 115 ], building upon decades of interest in identifying key aspects of design cognition and the support of design work through methods (e.g., References [ 21 , 104 , 134 ]). We draw on the definition given by Stolterman and colleagues [ 152 ] of design methods as “tools, techniques, and approaches that support design activity in [a] way that is appreciated by practicing interaction designers,”and Gray’s [ 88 ] definition that describes design methods as “tool[s] that allow designers to support thinking, reflecting and acting upon design activities.”Both definitions are consistent with historical framings of methods in the design studies literature, such as Cross’ [ 49 ] description of methods as “step-by-step, teach-able, learnable, repeatable, and communicable procedures to aid the designer in the course of designing.” We view methods as a form of design knowledge that does not function alone, but is rather activated through the designer’s activity and judgment, reflecting on the design knowledge contained within the methods [ 53 , 83 , 88 , 89 , 153 ]. This knowledge can be abstracted further to describe the repertoire of an individual designer, which includes both stores of existing design precedent [ 140 ] and larger assemblages of tool knowledge that Gray et al. [ 87 ] have previously referred to as a designer’s conceptual repertoire .

PIVONKA , Purdue University, West Lafayette, United States JINGNING CHEN , Purdue University, West Lafayette, United States Over the past decade, HCI researchers and practitioners have increasingly addressed ethics-focused issues through a range of theoretical, methodological, and pragmatic contributions to the field. While many forms of design knowledge have been proposed and described, we focus explicitly on knowledge that has been codified as “methods,”which we define as structured supports for everyday work practices of designers. In this article, we identify, analyze, and map a collection of 63 existing methods intentionally designed for ethical impact. Building on results of a content analysis of these methods, we contribute a descriptive record of how these methods operationalize ethics, their intended audience or context of use, their “core”or “script,”and the means by which these methods are formulated and codified. Building on these results, we provide an initial definition of ethics-focused methods, identifying potential opportunities for the development of future methods to support ethical design practice. Pivonka, and Jingning Chen. 2024. Surveying a Landscape of Ethics-Focused Design Methods. ACM J. Responsib. Comput.

2.3 Linking Design Methods and Ethics to Describe Ethics-Focused Methods In this section, we link and synthesize the previous two sections describing research on design methods and ethical practice, respectively, to support our survey of “ethics-focused methods.” We define an ethics-focused method as a design method that directly contains or references one or more sensitizing concepts or theoretical commitments that relate to values and/or ethics and, in doing so, inscribes or operationalizes ethical concerns into the codified method. The resulting collection of ethics-focused methods we survey and characterize in this article are differentiated from conventional design methods, conceptual frames, or theoretical commitments based on the explicit inclusion of ethical or value-related commitments that cannot be easily separated from the core procedural knowledge embedded in the method.

We identified two kinds of existing frameworks that were used: (1) established design methods; and (2) other standalone methods. Not all methods used existing frameworks, with only 25 out of the 63 methods representing this behavior. Established design methods that were referenced (as listed in the Universal Methods of Design [ 98 ]) included: personas [ 40 , 122 ], cognitive walkthrough [ 96 ], stakeholder map [ 172 ], scenarios [ 22 , 130 , 170 ], experience mapping [ 148 ], cultural probes [ 174 ], qualitative research interviews (used as required technique in [ 22 , 52 , 128 ]), and ethnography (used as a basic methodology for Ethnographically Informed [ 129 ]). These methods build upon or extend existing methods or approaches, facilitating the use of these methods with little prior preparation and expert knowledge. Other standalone methods include less common methods, and frequently ethics-focused methods, with their own mechanics that are used in one of the methods we analyzed. Examples include: Ethics Canvas (another ethics focused method [ 136 ], which was used to inspire and build Data Ethics Canvas [ 3 ]); Design Heuristics (a card deck [ 5 ] which was used in the cognitive walkthrough approach in the Idea Generation through Empathy method [ 96 ]); Ethical Disclaimer (another method which was used as an input in Ethical Contract [ 75 ]); Linkography (used as a baseline framework [ 81 ] in Ethicography [ 44 ]); and a combination of Value Scenarios [ 130 ], Envisioning Cards [ 67 ], and Value Sketch [ 166 ] (used to build and follow steps in the Value Sensitive Action-Reflection Model [ 174 ]). These methods require user(s)’ existing knowledge to complete the method, including the functioning of other related method(s). Design Process Implementation.

Thus, in building and elaborating the inherent structures of existing design methods, we are able to point towards a conceptual repertoire that is implicit in both design knowledge and use. This notion of a conceptual repertoire also builds on previous work in the HCI and design communities that has interrogated both prescribed and performative accounts of design practices, including both the exploration and performance of methods by practitioners from Goodman and colleagues [ 82 , 83 ] and Reeves [ 135 ], and the differentiation between codification and performance proposed by Gray [ 88 ]. In this article, we specifically leverage the definitional work by Gray [ 88 , 89 ] in characterizing the knowledge contained within design methods, with our analytic focus for this study drawing only on their articulation of prescriptive and presentation-oriented stances toward methods. A prescriptive or codification-oriented stance “reveals the extent to which procedural and descriptive knowledge is bound up in the method itself,”while a presentation-oriented stance “describes how the method is communicated, packaged, and disseminated, focusing on the ways in which methods are articulated to their anticipated audiences”[ 89 ]. We do not seek to characterize these methods in relation to their performance, defined as “aspects of a method that are revealed only as the method is used in a particular context by a designer, often with implicit connections to the codified form of the method”[ 89 ], since this is a space already investigated by numerous HCI and STS scholars (e.g., References [ 83 , 135 , 144 , 150 ]).

We also build upon prior work that has defined and curated a range of methods to support design activity, with such work having a stated goal of describing methods in ways that are simple enough for designers to adapt, apply and combine different methods in various ways [ 51 ]. Löwgren and Stolterman [ 115 ] have built upon this notion of method reuse, stating that methods should be accessible, flexible, and adaptable for designers to apply either independently or alongside the designer’s current “toolbox”in different contexts. Further, Stolterman [ 151 ] has claimed that any knowledge introduced into design practice should bear a “rationality resonance,”whereby the content of methods should resonate with the complexity of practice. Building upon this framing of building resonance into design methods, multiple scholars have sought to create and curate design method collections, including: methods for creativity and innovation to improve the range of design production [ 30 , 126 ]; a collection of UX evaluation methods [ 162 ]; a classification of methods, including traditional, adapted, innovative methods, and methods for interpretation and analysis [ 97 ]; a popular collection of UX research and design methods for design students and practitioners titled Universal Methods of Design [ 98 ]; a design kit for Human-Centered Design practice by

Surveying a Landscape of Ethics-Focused Design Methods 22:7 In combining these constructs in this way, we treat ethics as a stance, theory, or lens through which values can be operationalized and contend that these elements that relate to moral philosophy can be embedded into design supports, including methods. One foundational definition of ethics and values in design we have used during the process of identification and analysis of the collection of methods comes from STS scholar Peter-Paul Verbeek, who states a commitment: “[...] to analyze the moral aspects of technology design in a systematic way”by taking into account “the social impact that the technology in design will have as soon as it enters society ”[ 161 , p. 361]. We specifically build on Verbeek’s call to build and codify ethical supports that anticipate the social impact that results from design activity. 3 Our Approach To map the landscape of existing ethics-focused methods, we collected a total of 63 methods and conducted a qualitative content analysis [ 102 , 131 ] to describe the knowledge contained in these methods. This content analysis included the characterization of these methods on various levels and dimensions that will be detailed below as a part of our analysis approach. The research questions addressed through this article are as follows: (1) What design methods have an ethical focus, and how are ethical considerations operationalized in these methods? (2) Who are the intended audience(s) for these methods? (3) How are these methods described? All outcomes of our analysis process follow FAIR practices [ 164 ], ensuring that our data outcomes are Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, and Reusable . We have deposited our method coding outcomes as an archival dataset [ 46 ].

Surveying a Landscape of Ethics-Focused Design Methods 22:17 the user to assess their design through quantitative scores, ethical scores, design requirements, reflections, or other evaluation metrics. For instance, to illustrate the range of evaluation results, Ethical Design Scorecards [ 63 ] provide an “ethical score” for the users to indicate the potential ethical valence of their design decision, and Re-Shape [ 143 ] allows computer science students to evaluate their own decision making in the form of a reflection of their responsibilities towards data. Methods with values as an output define a new mindset or outlook on what elements or abstract principles are most important in the users’ design process. Methods with outputs such as users/stakeholders and user information have a similar definition as when they are used as inputs; however, as outputs the information about users is realized and produced through the methods. An output of procedural information encompasses relevant and possible next steps and a future plan of action for decision making. Finally, methods with research outcomes include artifacts produced using the method that are possible sources of future research or analysis, such as design research artifacts (e.g., user stories in speculative enactments [ 61 ]) or co-design materials (e.g., in the Value Sensitive Action-Reflection Model [ 174 ]). Existing frameworks/methods used. We sought to identify any existing design methods or frameworks that methods were built on, relied upon, or referenced. These frameworks are not translated into the method for the user, but rather they require the user to directly interact with these frameworks in order to successfully implement the ethics-focused method in their work.

It is claimed that these strategies and methodologies can “open new conversations about social values and encourage consensus around those values as design criteria”[ 144 ]; identify new ways to expose and reflect upon designers’ responsibility or attitudes toward value-based decisions [ 93 , 95 ]; propose suggestions for critical and reflective technical practice [ 20 ]; foreground tools for value comprehension in particular contexts [ 68 ]; provide practitioners with ethical codes for computing work [ 1 ]; frame policies for ethical responsibility for organizations [ 80 ]; and offer requirements for ethics curriculum for computing and engineering education [ 84 , 101 ]. This range of prior work illustrates the efforts of the HCI, STS, and design communities toward identifying opportunities for supporting ethically-focused work practices. However, as an additional point of complication, portions of this prior work has been critiqued regarding its lack of resonance in authentic work settings, or due to the lack of adequate translation of these practices from academia to practice [ 91 , 117 , 145 ]. Finally, while collections of methods have become commonplace in the last decade, with texts such as Universal Methods of Design [ 98 ] now in regular use by practitioners and educators, none of these collections appear to include even an implicit focus on the ethical content of methods. In this article, we focus our efforts on surveying the landscape of ethics literature through the framing of design methods, with the goal of gathering and characterizing the existing landscape of ethics-focused and value-conscious design methods.

Chivukula et al. 3.2 Data Analysis Using the method descriptions as our unit of analysis, we describe our data analysis procedures in three broad steps, as guided by Neuendorf’s qualitative content analysis approach [ 131 ]: (1) familiarizing ourselves with the data set; (2) creating and validating the coding scheme; and (3) performing open and axial coding. Our approach in using content analysis [ 131 ] involved a process of constant reflexivity and researcher alignment, given the complexity of the method and the ill-defined nature of our topic of interest. All researchers involved in this process have taken design and qualitative research methods coursework, and/or were involved in previous research projects that used content analysis or similar qualitative or critical analysis methods. Additionally, all researchers had prior experience engaging with conventional design methods through classroom projects and/or professional design work. These experiences enabled our research team to identify and characterize these methods, as we collectively brought knowledge of a broad spectrum of ethics-focused knowledge and design expertise. We reflexively engaged in open and axial coding as a key part of our content analysis process, where we first began to code for all potential aspects of method prescription (open coding) and later identified meaningful abstractions of these codes (axial coding) to form robust answers to our research questions.

discussion_full:
[Discussion paragraph 1] In this article, we have constructed a range of descriptors of ethics-focused methods that allow us to identify existing mechanisms for ethical support, along with opportunities for the development, adaptation, and dissemination of new methods. In this section, we return to the three stances towards design methods proposed by Gray  to characterize our contribution to the literature, consider factors relating to method resonance in current and future ethics-focused methods, and describe other opportunities for future research in this area. First, in Section 5.1 , we outline how the findings represent a codification-focused foundation for describing ethics-focused methods and point toward opportunities for new method development. Second, in Section 5.2 we build upon our codification-focused analysis to identify how our novel method vocabulary might be used to consider issues of method resonance, including the performance of these methods in complex organizational contexts. Third, in Section 5.3 , we conclude with a call for research and design work that engages ethics as a key dimension of design practice, laying the foundation for future scholarship that connects method design, method use, and method performance.   range of ethics-focused methods—many never before addressed in the academic literature, and none brought together as a collection previously beyond methodological “families” such as the VSD methods.

[Discussion paragraph 2] This collection, in itself, represents a substantial contribution to HCI and technology ethics scholarship, allowing the collection to continue to grow, and providing a novel analytic vocabulary to describe and frame both method knowledge in general and ethics-focused method knowledge in particular. When viewing our findings through a prescriptive- and presentation-oriented stance, we are able to use this initial landscape of ethicsfocused methods to identify both areas of strong existing support and opportunities for new method development.

[Discussion paragraph 3] Surveying a Landscape of Ethics-Focused Design Methods 22:21 By identifying possible paradigmatic combinations of input (I), mechanics (M), and output (O) (building on results from Section 4.3.1 ), we can identify areas of current method saturation and areas where new opportunities for ethical support could be considered. From our analysis, we have shown that common areas for ethics-focused method support include: (1) evaluation of existing design artifacts that lead to design opportunities; and (2) concept generation that is driven by evaluation of the problem frame and related constraints. Additionally, as shown in Table 3 , it is evident that the majority of methods we analyzed were research-focused (Phases 2 and 4), generatively-focused (Phase 3 and 4), or evaluation-focused (Phase 4). These common patterns of method support also call attention to areas where little method support is currently focused. For example, there are virtually no methods that address Phase 1 activities, even though this phase is where a space for ethically-focused work is formed by a design team, including the inscription of the problem space with ethical concerns. One of the rare example of methods involved in this phase includes the Ethical Contract method , which enables a discussion among all the stakeholders on the project to clearly discuss and divide their “ethical responsibilities”prior to concept generation. This method, and others that might fit into this early stage of design work, shows potential in discussing, communicating, and formalizing ethical responsibilities across multiple stakeholders in everyday practice—resonating with findings from Shilton, Wong, and Gray regarding the situated and collaborative nature of ethics in practice [ 91 , 145 , 168 ].

[Discussion paragraph 4] Similarly, new methods in Phase 5 could aid designers to engage in continuous evaluation of their work in its final social context. Methods that engage designers and technologists in an ethical focus in Phase 5 could be particularly relevant for contemporary technology ethics challenges that are often only fully realized when technologies are adopted at scale; for instance, the challenges brought by the creation and use of large language models or the impacts of surveillance techniques that build upon widespread data capture. The creation of new methods to address these spaces, or the intentional adaptation of existing methods to support particular areas of methodological underprovision could add additional supports to critical areas of practice. Future scholarship could also leverage methods or resources used in other critically-oriented design contexts such as participatory design. While participatory design practices rely upon a range of resources, PD practitioners do so by using a different framing and prescriptive format of methods than those we have sought to codify in this article. However, Hansen et al.’s  mapping of inputs, processes, and effects in PD might be useful in identifying where methods or method-like resources could be created and mapped onto the vocabulary we propose here (e.g., linking inputs, mechanisms, and effects to our vocabulary of inputs, mechanics, and outputs). As examples of this mapping, Phase 1 methods could be enriched through PD approaches to identify and deeply involve a range of stakeholders with implications for what it means to “participate”; similarly, critically engaging with questions relating to product launch and future use (Phase 5) might be supported by the PD concept of infrastructuring .

[Discussion paragraph 5] This expansion and inclusion of resources from these other contexts might also require different sampling strategies. In this article, while sampling the literature the presence of intact methods, we could have missed the presence of bespoke methods that were presented only as elements of broader case studies. We have observed elements of methods or entirely novel methods that include an ethical stance in PD and Research through Design literature which may not have been presented distinctly as “methods”and might be captured through future systematic or scoping reviews. Additionally, future method development could also leverage the analytic vocabulary we have constructed and described in this article. As articulated by Gray et al. , method designers leverage constraints to create methods that have specific types of focus and potential impact; thus, the vocabulary we describe here relating to method cores, mechanics, and other framing language may provide many new points of entry for the creation of new methods.

[Discussion paragraph 6] 22:22 S. S. Chivukula et al.  , we presented opportunities to continue to grow the current landscape of ethics-focused methods. However, there are also opportunities to support the ethical impact of these methods by considering potential performance in ecologically-bounded settings. In making this shift towards performance, we analytically distinguish between the codification-oriented focus of this survey of methods, which focuses on what “hooks” or potential “scripts” are contained within each method, and how these elements might be performed in direct or appropriated forms by practitioners. In doing so, we build on Gray et al.  notion of scaffolding as a means of building potential resonance with the demands of practice as well as Goodman et al.  reflections on complexity-in-practice , seeking to support the needs of practitioners in quickly identifying relevant methods, encouraging adaptation and combining of methods, and the creation of novel methods that address their emergent needs. Our analytic focus in this article was on the knowledge contained within the method as it is codified by the method designer , but the structure and characteristics of these methods also provides a script for the potential performance of these methods in everyday practice, including inscriptions of underlying beliefs about practitioners, practice, and available resources. These ecological elements will constrain or enable performance, and the degree to which methods consider these variables may lead to a higher or lower degree of ecological resonance .

[Discussion paragraph 7] Thus, if method designers do not consider ecological resonance factors, the methods they create may lack critical elements that allow them to be robustly and flexibly be used in everyday technology design practices. To complicate this notion of resonance further, different framings of ecologies may also lead to alternate perspectives on the role of tools and resources and their relationship to methods or methodologies. For instance, while our approach to technology and design practice has been methodology agnostic (reflecting the experiences of many of the practitioners we have engaged with in prior work), engaging with and supporting a design team that explicitly structures their work in relation to methodological commitments such as VSD or Participatory Design might result in the description and use of supports such as methods in different ways. In both of these methodological traditions, ethics or value-related commitments are typically made at the methodology level rather than the method or resource level, which may cause the prescriptive ethics component to be considered and framed in a different way. In future work, engaging with a range of such different classifications of resources to support ethical action (building upon those we list in Section 3.1.2 ) can aid researchers in identifying and expanding our current set of methods. In our collection, we focus on how the characteristics embedded in method descriptions foreshadow the potential resonance of these methods with the constraints and complexity of practice settings and the capacity and existing knowledge of the practitioners while also framing the situation in a ethically-valenced way.

[Discussion paragraph 8] Building on Stolterman’s  concept of rationality resonance , which highlights the relationship between suggested vs. existing practice, we can further question the barriers to adoption of these methods in practice settings, identifying spaces where the ethical knowledge of practitioners may be incomplete [ 72 , 120 ]; spaces where the agency and power of designers is not available to the extent that methods might assume [ 45 , 168 ]; and spaces where ethical complexity across multiple stakeholder positions is unaccounted for . There are numerous ways that performance may be impacted by method structure, but we provide an illustrative set of examples: —If the method core or mechanic is not easily and quickly understood by a practitioner, the method is unlikely to be performed at all, and any potential ethical impact may be entirely lost. —Methods may require practitioners to be able to evaluate their decisions based on concepts such as utility, human rights, and justice, which often resist quantification and may be

[Discussion paragraph 9] Surveying a Landscape of Ethics-Focused Design Methods 22:23 misunderstood or impossible without specialized ethics training. Method structures may also require designers to already have the vocabulary and capacity to express attitudes towards social responsibility and have the capability to take responsibility for positive social impact. If practitioners do not have these forms of applied ethical knowledge, their ability to perform elements of methods with these assumptions embedded in them would be compromised. —A method may require knowledge of another theory or method in order to understand its value and potential performance, which could limit the number of practitioners that could derive benefit. Depending on practitioners’ knowledge of relevant concepts embedded in a method, they may be unable to engage the method in its full complexity or perhaps may find no value at all. —A method might be ambiguous in ethical framing when viewed on its own (e.g., the use of card sorting, scenarios, or mockups) but take on an ethical character due to the overall methodological framing being used by a design team. Thus, a performance of method might rely on other types of framing knowledge or sensitizing concepts in order to make method use coherent and relating to ethical concerns. —The knowledge embedded in methods may be presented using types of guidance that are intended to encourage certain patterns of performance, while lacking guidance on how the method should be applied in relation to existing business forces or other forms of complexity beyond the designer themself. The degree to which the method itself is flexible, or anticipates elements of organizational or ecological complexity, may impact whether and how the method can be performed.

[Discussion paragraph 10] While we cannot resolve this issue of performance in relation to the methods we analyzed in this article, we propose that future researchers and method designers could address the role of methods as an emergent “new rationality” that could promote ethical practices, while also guarding against method descriptions and codification that lack resonance with the ethical design complexity present in everyday work practices. We also recognize, in keeping with the findings of other scholars [ 120 , 124 ], that the presence of methods or codes of ethics as the only intervention strategy cannot guarantee ethical outcomes; instead, viewing ethics as a mediated and continuous activity reveals the importance of practitioners’ knowledge and ability to act as well as the larger ecological setting of their work [ 35 , 91 ]. A full consideration of ecological resonance requires attention to specific methods, practitioner knowledge regarding ethics and methods, extant methodologies, and other ecological factors that may shape method awareness, activation, and evaluation of efficacy—and could be guided by recent work by Cash et al.  on evaluating method efficacy and effectiveness.  ication of both a collection of ethics-focused methods and creation of an analytic vocabulary to describe characteristics of these methods demarcates an important space for future research and design practices that can better describe and support ethical dimensions of design and technology work. First, this work serves as the beginning point for the intentional collection and curation of design methods with an ethical focus.

[Discussion paragraph 11] We call on scholars and practitioners to add to this collection through the addition of formal methods, further description of adaptation and performance of these methods in specific practice contexts, and identification of new types of ethical inscription beyond the method cores we have identified in our analysis. In particular, there are opportunities to translate supporting structures and resources from other ethically-focused methodologies such

[Discussion paragraph 12] 22:24 S. S. Chivukula et al. as Participatory Design, VSD, or Values at Play. While there may not be an exact mapping of vocabulary across these domains and the ethics-focused method collection we have built, there are numerous opportunities to identify and re-frame resources from these other traditions of criticallyinformed design that might have relevance as part of a methodology-agnostic collection. Second, this work provides a new language to describe knowledge contained within methods, building on the work of Gray , Daalhuizen , and others. This vocabulary has uptakes not only for building and extending a collection of ethics-focused methods, but also in providing more analytic precision in describing how methods intersect with practice in a performative stance. While this vocabulary is still in its early stages, building precision will allow for knowledge building to occur across different disciplinary spaces where ethical issues and supports are currently being discussed. In particular, we envision connecting philosophical discourses around ethics, characterizations of professional ethics and related educational content, and existing or novel supports to engage designers and technologists more deeply in ethically-focused decision making. Third, this work lays the foundation for both the creation of new methods and the identification of strategies to better inscribe ethical impact into methods. Future work can investigate and evaluate ideal spaces for new methodological support and compare differing combinations of cores, presentation formats, and/or mechanics for ethically-engaged design.

[Discussion paragraph 13] We also envision opportunities for scholars and designers to identify other productive mechanisms or re-framings of ethical design practices—for example, considering more activist perspectives (e.g., data feminism, design justice) and compliance perspectives (e.g., accessibility) as alternate paths into a broader ethical discourse.

We draw on the definition given by Stolterman and colleagues [ 152 ] of design methods as “tools, techniques, and approaches that support design activity in [a] way that is appreciated by practicing interaction designers,”and Gray’s [ 88 ] definition that describes design methods as “tool[s] that allow designers to support thinking, reflecting and acting upon design activities.”Both definitions are consistent with historical framings of methods in the design studies literature, such as Cross’ [ 49 ] description of methods as “step-by-step, teach-able, learnable, repeatable, and communicable procedures to aid the designer in the course of designing.” We view methods as a form of design knowledge that does not function alone, but is rather activated through the designer’s activity and judgment, reflecting on the design knowledge contained within the methods [ 53 , 83 , 88 , 89 , 153 ]. This knowledge can be abstracted further to describe the repertoire of an individual designer, which includes both stores of existing design precedent [ 140 ] and larger assemblages of tool knowledge that Gray et al. Surveying a Landscape of Ethics-Focused Design Methods SHRUTHI SAI CHI V UK ULA , Pratt Institute, New York City, United States COLIN GRAY , Indiana University, Bloomington, United States ZIQING LI , Purdue University, West Lafayette, United States ANNE C. PIVONKA , Purdue University, West Lafayette, United States JINGNING CHEN , Purdue University, West Lafayette, United States Over the past decade, HCI researchers and practitioners have increasingly addressed ethics-focused issues through a range of theoretical, methodological, and pragmatic contributions to the field. While many forms of design knowledge have been proposed and described, we focus explicitly on knowledge that has been codified as “methods,”which we define as structured supports for everyday work practices of designers. In this article, we identify, analyze, and map a collection of 63 existing methods intentionally designed for ethical impact. Building on results of a content analysis of these methods, we contribute a descriptive record of how these methods operationalize ethics, their intended audience or context of use, their “core”or “script,”and the means by which these methods are formulated and codified. Building on these results, we provide an initial definition of ethics-focused methods, identifying potential opportunities for the development of future methods to support ethical design practice. 2.3 Linking Design Methods and Ethics to Describe Ethics-Focused Methods In this section, we link and synthesize the previous two sections describing research on design methods and ethical practice, respectively, to support our survey of “ethics-focused methods.” We define an ethics-focused method as a design method that directly contains or references one or more sensitizing concepts or theoretical commitments that relate to values and/or ethics and, in doing so, inscribes or operationalizes ethical concerns into the codified method. The resulting collection of ethics-focused methods we survey and characterize in this article are differentiated from conventional design methods, conceptual frames, or theoretical commitments based on the explicit inclusion of ethical or value-related commitments that cannot be easily separated from the core procedural knowledge embedded in the method.

We identified two kinds of existing frameworks that were used: (1) established design methods; and (2) other standalone methods. Not all methods used existing frameworks, with only 25 out of the 63 methods representing this behavior. Established design methods that were referenced (as listed in the Universal Methods of Design [ 98 ]) included: personas [ 40 , 122 ], cognitive walkthrough [ 96 ], stakeholder map [ 172 ], scenarios [ 22 , 130 , 170 ], experience mapping [ 148 ], cultural probes [ 174 ], qualitative research interviews (used as required technique in [ 22 , 52 , 128 ]), and ethnography (used as a basic methodology for Ethnographically Informed [ 129 ]). These methods build upon or extend existing methods or approaches, facilitating the use of these methods with little prior preparation and expert knowledge. Other standalone methods include less common methods, and frequently ethics-focused methods, with their own mechanics that are used in one of the methods we analyzed. Examples include: Ethics Canvas (another ethics focused method [ 136 ], which was used to inspire and build Data Ethics Canvas [ 3 ]); Design Heuristics (a card deck [ 5 ] which was used in the cognitive walkthrough approach in the Idea Generation through Empathy method [ 96 ]); Ethical Disclaimer (another method which was used as an input in Ethical Contract [ 75 ]); Linkography (used as a baseline framework [ 81 ] in Ethicography [ 44 ]); and a combination of Value Scenarios [ 130 ], Envisioning Cards [ 67 ], and Value Sketch [ 166 ] (used to build and follow steps in the Value Sensitive Action-Reflection Model [ 174 ]). These methods require user(s)’ existing knowledge to complete the method, including the functioning of other related method(s). Design Process Implementation.

Thus, in building and elaborating the inherent structures of existing design methods, we are able to point towards a conceptual repertoire that is implicit in both design knowledge and use. This notion of a conceptual repertoire also builds on previous work in the HCI and design communities that has interrogated both prescribed and performative accounts of design practices, including both the exploration and performance of methods by practitioners from Goodman and colleagues [ 82 , 83 ] and Reeves [ 135 ], and the differentiation between codification and performance proposed by Gray [ 88 ]. In this article, we specifically leverage the definitional work by Gray [ 88 , 89 ] in characterizing the knowledge contained within design methods, with our analytic focus for this study drawing only on their articulation of prescriptive and presentation-oriented stances toward methods. A prescriptive or codification-oriented stance “reveals the extent to which procedural and descriptive knowledge is bound up in the method itself,”while a presentation-oriented stance “describes how the method is communicated, packaged, and disseminated, focusing on the ways in which methods are articulated to their anticipated audiences”[ 89 ]. We do not seek to characterize these methods in relation to their performance, defined as “aspects of a method that are revealed only as the method is used in a particular context by a designer, often with implicit connections to the codified form of the method”[ 89 ], since this is a space already investigated by numerous HCI and STS scholars (e.g., References [ 83 , 135 , 144 , 150 ]).

We also build upon prior work that has defined and curated a range of methods to support design activity, with such work having a stated goal of describing methods in ways that are simple enough for designers to adapt, apply and combine different methods in various ways [ 51 ]. Löwgren and Stolterman [ 115 ] have built upon this notion of method reuse, stating that methods should be accessible, flexible, and adaptable for designers to apply either independently or alongside the designer’s current “toolbox”in different contexts. Further, Stolterman [ 151 ] has claimed that any knowledge introduced into design practice should bear a “rationality resonance,”whereby the content of methods should resonate with the complexity of practice. Building upon this framing of building resonance into design methods, multiple scholars have sought to create and curate design method collections, including: methods for creativity and innovation to improve the range of design production [ 30 , 126 ]; a collection of UX evaluation methods [ 162 ]; a classification of methods, including traditional, adapted, innovative methods, and methods for interpretation and analysis [ 97 ]; a popular collection of UX research and design methods for design students and practitioners titled Universal Methods of Design [ 98 ]; a design kit for Human-Centered Design practice by

Surveying a Landscape of Ethics-Focused Design Methods 22:7 In combining these constructs in this way, we treat ethics as a stance, theory, or lens through which values can be operationalized and contend that these elements that relate to moral philosophy can be embedded into design supports, including methods. One foundational definition of ethics and values in design we have used during the process of identification and analysis of the collection of methods comes from STS scholar Peter-Paul Verbeek, who states a commitment: “[...] to analyze the moral aspects of technology design in a systematic way”by taking into account “the social impact that the technology in design will have as soon as it enters society ”[ 161 , p. 361]. We specifically build on Verbeek’s call to build and codify ethical supports that anticipate the social impact that results from design activity. 3 Our Approach To map the landscape of existing ethics-focused methods, we collected a total of 63 methods and conducted a qualitative content analysis [ 102 , 131 ] to describe the knowledge contained in these methods. This content analysis included the characterization of these methods on various levels and dimensions that will be detailed below as a part of our analysis approach. (2) Who are the intended audience(s) for these methods? (3) How are these methods described? All outcomes of our analysis process follow FAIR practices [ 164 ], ensuring that our data outcomes are Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, and Reusable .
